name: k — The Curvature Constant
definition:
k is the curvature constant that determines the shape of the toroidal manifold.
    It is the ratio of the minor radius to the major radius: k = r/R.
    
    This single parameter controls:
    - The thickness of the torus tube
    - The Gaussian curvature at each point
    - The volume of the manifold
    - The stability of the Harmonic Return

stability_implications.description: How k affects the stability of the manifold

small_k.effect: Thin torus. High curvature. Unstable. The manifold is prone to tearing.
small_k.gold_concentration: Gold concentrates at the outer edge (high K)
small_k.harmonic_return: Unstable. The helix pattern breaks down.

current_k.effect: Balanced torus. Moderate curvature. Stable. The optimal shape for the Universal Map.
current_k.harmonic_return: Stable. The helix pattern is well-defined.

large_k.effect: Fat torus. Low curvature. Over-stable. The manifold is too rigid.
large_k.harmonic_return: Over-damped. The helix pattern is too rigid.

the_admirals_choice.reasoning:
The Admiral has chosen k = 0.3 as the optimal curvature constant because:
        
        1. It produces a torus that is thick enough to contain complex structure
           but thin enough to maintain coherence.
        
        2. The Gaussian curvature varies sufficiently across the surface to create
           distinct regions (gold bands, nodes, voids).
        
        3. The Harmonic Return is stable — the helix pattern neither breaks down
           (small k) nor becomes too rigid (large k).
        
        4. The volume is large enough to contain the entire observable universe
           but small enough to be computationally tractable.
        
        5. The shape is aesthetically pleasing — it is the "golden" proportion
           of torus shapes, neither too thin nor too fat.
